front: Composer: Czech Romantic; directed the National Conservatory of Music in New York (1892–95), recruited by Jeanette Thurber for $15,000/year
work: General / Biographical

front: Composer: his first symphony 'The Bells of Zlonice' was submitted to a competition, presumed lost, and found in a Leipzig bookstore 18 years later
work: General / Biographical

front: Composer: his son-in-law and pupil Josef Suk wrote the Asrael Symphony in his memory
work: General / Biographical

front: Composer: most famous piano piece is the G-flat major Humoresque, seventh of a set of eight
work: General / Biographical

front: Symphony: Czech composer's final symphony in E minor, subtitled 'From the New World,' premiered by Anton Seidl at Carnegie Hall
work: Symphony No. 9 in E minor, 'From the New World'

front: Symphony: second movement (Largo) features an English horn pentatonic solo; later adapted as the song 'Goin' Home'
work: Symphony No. 9 in E minor, 'From the New World'

front: Symphony: Harry Burleigh, a Black singer who worked as a maintenance man at the National Conservatory to pay tuition, influenced its English horn solo with spirituals
work: Symphony No. 9 in E minor, 'From the New World'

front: Symphony: second movement opens with brass chords modulating from E major to D-flat major before the English horn enters
work: Symphony No. 9 in E minor, 'From the New World'

front: Symphony: formerly numbered No. 8 and No. 5 due to out-of-order publication; Neil Armstrong took a recording to the Moon
work: Symphony No. 9 in E minor, 'From the New World'

front: Concerto: B minor cello concerto inspired by Victor Herbert's Second Cello Concerto; soloist enters after an 86-bar introduction
back: Cello Concerto in B minor, Op. 104 (Dvořák)
work: Cello Concerto in B minor, Op. 104

front: Concerto: dedicatee Hanuš Wihán wrote a cadenza Dvořák refused to include; Leo Stern performed the premiere after receiving a gift of two rare pigeons
back: Cello Concerto in B minor, Op. 104 (Dvořák)
work: Cello Concerto in B minor, Op. 104

front: Concerto: third movement quotes the song 'Leave Me Alone' as tribute to the composer's sister-in-law
back: Cello Concerto in B minor, Op. 104 (Dvořák)
work: Cello Concerto in B minor, Op. 104

front: Concerto: soloist enters with notes B, C-sharp, D-sharp, B in the first movement
back: Cello Concerto in B minor, Op. 104 (Dvořák)
work: Cello Concerto in B minor, Op. 104

front: String Quartet: opens with two violins oscillating on an F-major triad, then viola plays a syncopated pentatonic melody; written in Spillville, Iowa
work: String Quartet No. 12 in F major, 'American'

front: String Quartet: third movement transcribes a red-eyed vireo's call heard in Spillville; sometimes misidentified as a scarlet tanager
work: String Quartet No. 12 in F major, 'American'

front: Suite: 16 dances originally for piano four-hands, orchestrated; commissioned by Fritz Simrock at Brahms's urging; modeled on Brahms's Hungarian Dances
work: Slavonic Dances

front: Suite: uses the furiant dance (notable for 'horizontal hemiola') in place of a scherzo
work: Slavonic Dances

front: Tone Poem: four tone poems based on Karel Erben's Czech folklore collection Kytice — Water Goblin, Noon Witch, Golden Spinning Wheel, Wild Dove
work: Symphonic Poems (after Erben's Kytice)

front: Tone Poem: its goblin character is represented by a motif of three repeated F-sharps; near the end he throws a dead baby against a door
work: Symphonic Poems (after Erben's Kytice)

front: Tone Poem: the Slavic demon Lady Midday comes to steal a misbehaving child; the mother faints over him and accidentally smothers him
work: Symphonic Poems (after Erben's Kytice)

front: Work: six-movement piano trio in which each movement alternates between a solemn slow section and a fast bright section, based on the Ukrainian dumka form
work: Dumky Trio (Piano Trio No. 4)

front: Work: first three movements played without pause; opens with cello on C, E, A with piano descending sixths in triplets
work: Dumky Trio (Piano Trio No. 4)
